text:
O  Bulk power systems exhibit a range of dynamic oscillatory
.
h behaviors governed by their underlying modes of oscillation.
t
Low frequency oscillations associated with the small-signal
a
(or linear) stability of large power systems are undesirable
m
[ as they restrict maximum power transfer [3], [4], [5]. These
 
  oscillations are often classified by modes, which represent
1 specific patterns of oscillatory behavior (see Section II for
v
precise definition). For instance, local-mode oscillations arise
3
when generating units within an area oscillate relative to each
2
0 other. Instead, inter-area-mode oscillations, the most crucial
4 and detrimental, occur when large groups of generators in
0 different regions swing against each other.
.
4  When disturbances trigger  these oscillations in a power
0 system,  local or wide-area measurements can capture their
5 effects. It is very essential for system operators to accurately
2
: determine which machines or local control units—such as
v automatic voltage regulators (AVRs) or power system stabi-
i
lizers (PSS)—are most impacted by the mode. This means
X
r understanding whether a machine is being heavily disturbed
a by the mode or is contributing significantly to it. Such insight
allows operators to take prompt corrective actions, like taking
a machine or unit offline, to restore stability [6].
A widely used diagnostic tool for identifying which states,
such as rotor angle and speed,  in a generator (and thus its
location) are impacted by an mode is the mode participation
factor. These factors are defined by the product of the left and
right eigenvectors of the system’s state matrix corresponding
to the mode’s eigenvalue. They quantify a state’s contribution
to a mode. High participation in a controllable state suggests
R.Anguluri is with the Department of Computer Science and Electrical
Engineering, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, MD 85281, USA.
A. Al Maruf is with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering,
California State University, Los Angeles, CA 90032, USA (emails: rajan-
gul@umbc.edu and amaruf@calstatela.edu). The authors thank ChatGPT for
improving the clarity and conciseness of the sentences in this paper.

Observability Blocking Controllers
Networks
d Abdullah Al Maruf
that control actions, such as AVR/PSS tuning, can mitigate
the mode, while high participation value in an observable
state indicates that the mode can be effectively detected using
wide-area measurements (see [7], [8], [9], [10])
Recent cyber-physical incidents, including both adversarial
and natural disturbances, have demonstrated that power grids
are vulnerable to targeted disruptions [11], [12], [13]. Ma-
licious attackers can exploit vulnerabilities in measurement
systems and control  loops amplifying destabilizing oscilla-
tions or misdirecting corrective actions. Similarly, extreme
weather events and grid contingencies can induce shifts in
inter-area dynamics. These challenges necessitate power en-
gineers to understand the reliability or fragility of monitoring
metrics such as participation factors and observing inter-area
modes in tie-line power flows.
We take a first step toward in this direction to understand
the extent  to which adversaries can change participation
factors of any given mode and block observability of inter-
area-modes  in  tie-lie power flow measurements without
destabilizing the system. Our approach leverages recently de-
veloped state-feedback controllers for blocking observability
and controllability in multi-agent networks systems [1], [2].
For an interconnected power system modeled as a linear-
time invariant small signal model, we develop a static state-
feedback matrix, which, when applied to the power system,
results in a closed-loop system with the following attributes:
(i)  the modes remain invariant but the participation factors
of selective modes are modified, or
(ii)  the modes remain invariant but  the observable inter-
area modes in open-loop system are unobservable.
We validate the performance our closed-loop controller on
a 3-machine, 9-bus system and a 16-machine, 68-bus system.
Our  results uncover some interesting behavior  in altering
mode participation factors. In particular, we can block the
participation of any desired state in any desired mode, but
this comes with a price. The state can now highly participate
in unexpected modes. We also discuss the reasons behind this
behavior (see Section V).
The rest of the paper is as follows. In Section II, we give
a brief introduction to modal analysis in power systems. In
Section III, we describe our research problem and in Section
IV we present our algorithms for  the design of blocking
controllers. In Section V, we provide simulation results and
conclude the paper in Section VI.

metadata.section_title: A Primer on Power System Dynamics

text:
Since our interest is in the time scale where low-frequency
oscillations manifest in an interconnected power system, we

We make two assumptions. First, a centralized authority
can jointly actuate all available control inputs (e.g., AVR set
points or PSS inputs). This assumption is well-justified and
commonly used to mitigate inter-area oscillations through co-
ordination of multiple local controllers [15]. Second, all state
variables are measurable. While restrictive, this assumption is
reasonable when using a reduced-order or aggregated model
[16]. With these assumptions in place, we address:
(i)  for a given set of modes (local or inter-area), design a
full-state feedback controller to block participation of
a few select states in those modes.
(ii) design a full-state feedback controller to block observ-
ability of inter-area mode in tie-line flows.
Mathematically, the first question is to enforce pki = 0 for
a specific state k and a mode i, effectively eliminating that
state’s participation in the mode. The second objective aims
to set oji = 0 for all inter-area modes i in the j-th tie-line
power flow measurements.

Using our previous results on the surgical eigenstructure
assignment problem [1], [2], [17] we present algorithms to
compute a full-state feedback control law u(t) = Fx(t) to (i)
shape participation factors and (ii) enforce unobservability in
tie-line power flow measurements for given modes.
A. Blocking Participation Factors
Suppose that we want m states to not participate in the
mode λi of the state matrix A. Then our algorithm outputs
a state feedback matrix F such that pk1i = pk2i = · · · =
pkmi = 0 for the mode λi associated with the closed-loop
matrix (A+BF). We emphasize that our state feedback
keeps the mode λi invariant while modifying the k1-th, k2-
th,  · · · , km-th entries of  the open-loop right eigenvector
vi  to become zero. These zero entries in the eigenvector
will enforce zero participation of corresponding states  in
the mode λi. Below we present our algorithm for blocking
participation factors in a complex conjugate mode pair. For
more details, see [1].
Algorithm 1:
1) Select a pair of complex conjugate mode λi and λi+1
of A and its associated eigenvector vi and vi+1 where i ∈
{1, . . . , n − 1}. Note, λ  = λ¯  and v  = v¯ .

We assume that the matrix Vˆ constructed in Step (5) is
invertible. If not, additional steps must be taken to guarantee
invertibility. We refer the reader to [1] for details on this. If
m+2 ≤ q, then according to [1], Algorithm 1 is guaranteed
to find the desired F to block participation.

B. Blocking Observability for Tie-Line Power Flow
Blocking observability for  the tie-line power flow y(t)
corresponds to modifying open-loop eigenvector vi of A to
vˆi so that Cvi = 0 where vˆi  is the modified eigenvector
of (A+BF) corresponding to the mode λi. This operation
will render the mode λi to become unobservable according
to the PBH test [18]. We present the algorithm for blocking
observability in a pair of complex conjugate modes.
Algorithm 2:
1) Same as Step 1 in Algorithm 1.
2) Same as Step 2 in Algorithm 1.
3) Find the matrix M1 whose columns form a basis of
the null space of C. Then construct the matrix M2(λi) =
[N1(λi) M1]. Find the matrix M3(λi) whose columns form
a basis of the null space of M2(λi). Then hi is obtained by
the first q entries of any columns of M3(λi).
4-6) Steps 4-6 are the same as for Algorithm 1.
As before, we assume that  the matrix Vˆ constructed in
Step (5) is invertible. If not, additional steps must be taken
to guarantee invertibility (see [1]). Using the arguments in
[1], we can show that if rank(C) + 2 ≤ q, then Algorithm
2 is guaranteed to find the desired F to block observability.
An important point here is that Step 3 of Algorithm 2 is
different than the algorithms in [1] as here we allow a more
general structure for C matrix. In our algorithm, we compute
vˆi in a way so that it lies in the intersection of the null space
of C and assignable eigenvector space N1(λi). We further
note that our algorithms maintain the eigenvalues and right
eigenvectors except vˆi and vˆi+1, therefore they can be used
repeatedly to block observability or participation in multiple
modes.
V. PARTICIPATION AND INTER-AREA-OBSERVABILITY
BLOCKING IN POWER SYSTEMS
We use Algorithms 1 and 2 in Section IV to block selected
mode participation factors and specific inter-area modes in
the tie-line power flows of a (i) 3-machine, 9-bus system and
(ii) a 16-machine, 68-bus system. For both systems, at an
appropriate operating point, eliminating algebraic variables
yields the state-space models given in (2). To highlight the
impact of our control method, for the three machine system,
we work with the analytical Heffron-Phillips model.
A.

model  for each machine equipped with a local PSS unit.

metadata.section_title: Participation and inter-area-observability blocking in power systems

text:
We use Algorithms 1 and 2 in Section IV to block selected
mode participation factors and specific inter-area modes in
the tie-line power flows of a (i) 3-machine, 9-bus system and
(ii) a 16-machine, 68-bus system. For both systems, at an
appropriate operating point, eliminating algebraic variables
yields the state-space models given in (2). To highlight the
impact of our control method, for the three machine system,
we work with the analytical Heffron-Phillips model.
A. Case 1: 3 machine, 9 bus power system:
We adopt the presentation in [19], assuming a fourth-order
model  for each machine equipped with a local PSS unit.

This paper proposes full-state feedback control schemes
to modify and suppress mode participation factors and inter-
area mode observability in interconnected power systems.
The control algorithm leverages the classical eigenstructure
assignment method. Through detailed simulations on a 3-
machine, 9-bus system and a 16-machine, 68-bus system,
we demonstrate that our approach effectively reveals funda-
mental limits in power systems relying on modal analysis.
However, a drawback of this study is that full-state feed-
back is impractical in modern power systems. This highlights
the need for future research on developing output feedback
or decentralized control strategies for blocking mode partic-
ipation factors and observability.
